coredata.dc:title: Detection of tyrosinase in living cells using an Enteromorpha Prolifera based fluorescent probe
coredata.dc:creator: Dai, Qi
Wang, Xiaojuan
Liu, Cai
Feng, Zhenzhen
Ge, Baosheng
Ma, Xiqi
Zhang, Zhixiong
Yu, Jinyi
Wang, Xiaoqiang
Huang, Fang
coredata.dc:description: Melanoma, the skin cancer with the highest mortality rate, can be diagnosed at the early stage by detecting unique biomarkers. Over-expressed tyrosinase has been confirmed by dozens of clinical studies as an independent factor to evaluate the malignancy of melanoma. Using Enteromorpha Prolifera as the raw material, herein we develop a novel fluorescent probe, ECDY, which can sensitively detect the tyrosinase activity in different types of cells. More importantly, melanoma cells can be specifically distinguished through cell lysate measurements as well as the whole-cell imaging technique. Mechanically, the tyrosine groups on the surface of ECDY can be specifically recognized by tyrosinase and further converted into dopaquinone, which consequently causes the intramolecular fluorescence quenching of the probe through photoinduced electron transfer (PET). Tyrosinase can be detected within 20 min in the solution, and the detection limit is as low as 0.067 U mL−1. For the in vitro demonstration, we evaluate the fluorescence decay of ECDY in response to the intracellular tyrosinase activity within the lysate of various cell lines, including non-cancerous, non-melanoma cancerous, and mouse melanoma ones. The experimental results verify that ECDY can accurately measure the apparent tyrosinase activity in different cell lines and detect melanoma cell lysate specifically. The confocal fluorescence imaging experiments further demonstrate that ECDY can distinguish melanoma cells from others significantly. We believe that ECDY provides a new strategy for the efficient detection of tyrosinase and melanoma cells, and is expected to apply as a clinical diagnosis platform.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Melanoma
Tyrosinase
Fluorescent probe
Living cells
Intramolecular quenching

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence response of ECDY to tyrosinase
In vitro detection of melanoma cells
In vivo melanoma cells distinguishing from others

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Synthesis diagram of ECDY. (B) TEM image of ECDY. The inset is the HR-TEM image of ECDY. (C) The particle size statistics of ECDY based on TEM. (D) The hydration diameter distribution of ECD and ECDY measured by dynamic light scattering.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of ECD and ECDY (λ ex = 390 nm). The inset is the photographs of ECD and ECDY solution under daylight and a UV lamp (365 nm). (B) FT-IR spectra of ECD and ECDY. (C) UV–Vis absorption spectra of ECD, ECDY, mixture of ECD and l-tyrosine methyl ester, and free l-tyrosine methyl ester.

(A) Schematic diagram of ECDY detection of tyrosinase. (B) Fluorescence spectra of ECDY after mixing with different tyrosinase samples for 20 min. (C) Fluorescence spectra of ECDY after mixing with tyrosinase (10.0 U mL−1) for different duration times. (D) Time-dependent fluorescence decay of ECDY after mixing with different tyrosinase samples. (λ ex = 390 nm, λ em = 465 nm).

(A) Correlation between tyrosinase activity and fluorescence intensity of ECDY. The inset is the photographs of the typical mixture solution samples taken under daylight and a UV lamp (365 nm). (B) The fitting curve and regression equation between F/F 0 and tyrosinase activity over a linear range from 5 to 750 U L−1. (λ ex = 390 nm, λ em = 465 nm, n = 3).

(A) ECDY fluorescence intensity after mixing with various interfering organic compounds (protein 1.0 mg mL−1, enzyme 10.0 U mL−1) for 20 min. (B) The fluorescence intensity change of ECDY after mixing with various cell lysates for 30 min (λ ex = 390 nm, λ em = 465 nm, n = 3; ∗ indicated p < 0.05, ∗∗ indicated p < 0.01, ∗∗∗ indicated p < 0.001; - indicated tyrosinase negative, + indicated tyrosinase weakly positive, +++ indicated tyrosinase strongly positive, n = 3).

(A) Confocal microscopy imaging of different cells after incubation with ECDY and ECD, respectively, for 4 h. (B) The numerical plot of ECDY fluorescence signal collected in different cells in confocal microscopy images. (F 0 is the fluorescence intensity with ECD added, - indicated tyrosinase negative, + indicated tyrosinase weakly positive, +++ indicated tyrosinase strongly positive, n = 3).
Comparison of ECDY with other tyrosinase probes.

head.ce:title: Detection of tyrosinase in living cells using an Enteromorpha Prolifera based fluorescent probe

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil Processing, College of Chemical Engineering, China University of Petroleum (East China), Qingdao, 266580, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil Processing

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil Processing, College of Chemical Engineering, China University of Petroleum (East China), Qingdao 266580, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Technical Center of Qingdao Customs District, 99 Huanghe East Road, Huangdao District, 266500, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Technical Center of Qingdao Customs District

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Technical Center of Qingdao Customs District, 99 Huanghe East Road, Huangdao District, 266500, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Melanoma, the skin cancer with the highest mortality rate, can be diagnosed at the early stage by detecting unique biomarkers. Over-expressed tyrosinase has been confirmed by dozens of clinical studies as an independent factor to evaluate the malignancy of melanoma. Using Enteromorpha Prolifera as the raw material, herein we develop a novel fluorescent probe, ECDY, which can sensitively detect the tyrosinase activity in different types of cells. More importantly, melanoma cells can be specifically distinguished through cell lysate measurements as well as the whole-cell imaging technique. Mechanically, the tyrosine groups on the surface of ECDY can be specifically recognized by tyrosinase and further converted into dopaquinone, which consequently causes the intramolecular fluorescence quenching of the probe through photoinduced electron transfer (PET). Tyrosinase can be detected within 20 min in the solution, and the detection limit is as low as 0.067 U mL−1. For the in vitro demonstration, we evaluate the fluorescence decay of ECDY in response to the intracellular tyrosinase activity within the lysate of various cell lines, including non-cancerous, non-melanoma cancerous, and mouse melanoma ones. The experimental results verify that ECDY can accurately measure the apparent tyrosinase activity in different cell lines and detect melanoma cell lysate specifically. The confocal fluorescence imaging experiments further demonstrate that ECDY can distinguish melanoma cells from others significantly. We believe that ECDY provides a new strategy for the efficient detection of tyrosinase and melanoma cells, and is expected to apply as a clinical diagnosis platform.

ce:section.ce:para: Melanoma is a highly aggressive malignant tumor developed from melanocyte lesions [1]. In recent decades, the incidence of melanoma has continued to rise, leading to the highest mortality rate of skin cancers [2]. The primary diagnosis of melanoma mainly depends on clinical observation, skin biopsy, and histopathology [3,4]. Benefiting from the development of bioanalytical technologies, novel assays based on biomarker detection, spectral measurement, ultrasonic testing, and statistical data analysis have been constructed [5–12]. It is reported that the survival rate of patients with early melanoma (i.e., non-metastatic melanoma) can be as high as 97% when timely surgical treatments are received, whereas the therapy of metastatic melanoma is always very difficult [13]. Therefore, the early diagnosis and treatment are crucial and the development of primary melanoma detection methodology is highly demanded [14–16].

Tyrosinase (TYR) is a copper-containing oxidase that exists in melanocytes, which is mainly involved in two reactions associated with the synthesis of melanin, the monophenol hydroxylation and the conversion of catechol to o-quinone [17]. Although several more step reactions are required for the final production of melanin, tyrosinase is the rate-limiting enzyme in the whole process [18]. Studies have revealed that tyrosinase is overexpressed in melanoma cells [19], which consequently is used as a specific biomarker for melanoma diagnosis. So far, dozens of studies have confirmed the importance of tyrosinase in the clinical diagnosis of melanoma [20,21].

In recent years, a diverse range of tyrosinase activity assays have emerged, which are based on colorimetric methods [22,23], electrochemical methods [24–26], fluorescence methods [27–29], and Raman spectroscopic methods [30]. Among them, the methods based on fluorescence variation have been widely used due to the unique properties, and their applications for the tyrosinase activity analysis in solution have been extensively studied [31–33]. Wang and coworkers simply mixed upconversion-fluorescent nanoparticles and tyramine to construct a sensing system that could quickly (30 min) detect tyrosinase in solution [34]. However, the weakly alkaline working condition limited its application for the biological sample measurements. Besides, this binary tyrosinase detection system is not suitable for living cell detection. There is no doubt that compared to solution measurement the analysis of tyrosinase activity in living cells is more challenging. Hu and coworkers constructed a small molecular fluorescent probe by linking the 3-hydroxybenzyloxy unit to fluorescein, and applied this probe to detect tyrosinase in different kinds of cells [35]. However, this probe has not been applied to distinguish melanoma cells. Meanwhile, the high cost of complicated preparation process also prevents it from being widely used. Taking into account the complexity of the intracellular environment, the fluorescent probe applicable for biological detection of melanoma cells should possess significant selectivity for tyrosinase, good biocompatibility, and high cell uptake efficiency.

Carbon Dots (CDs), as a new group of fluorescent nanomaterials, have attracted intensive attention due to the excellent properties such as various functional groups, large specific surface area, and low biotoxicity. In recent years, CDs have been widely used for biological imaging [36], chemical sensing [37], and drug delivery [38]. In a previous work, we have successfully used Enteromorpha Prolifera as the raw material to prepare the biomass-based carbon dot (ECD) with high fluorescence stability and good biocompatibility [39]. It is noticed that the fluorescence of ECD is inert to phenols but could be quickly quenched by the quinones generated from the oxidation of phenols, indicating that it has the potential to detect tyrosinase with high sensitivity.

In this study, we develop a novel fluorescent probe, ECDY, based on the previously reported ECD. It is demonstrated that ECDY is highly sensitive to the activity of tyrosinase and can be used as a promising diagnostic platform for melanoma cell detection through the cell lysate measurement as well as the whole cell imaging technique.

All the chemicals and reagents were of analytical grade and used without further purification. In all experiments, the solutions were prepared using ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ) purified on the Millipore system (Millipore, USA). Mushroom tyrosinase and 1-Ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide (EDC) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Shanghai). l-tyrosine methyl ester, 1,4-quinone, and all metal ions were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Company. N-Hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) was purchased from Alfa Aesar (Tianjin) Chemical.

Enteromorpha Prolifera carbon dots (ECD) were prepared on the MX-4000 microwave rapid digestion system from Preekem Scientific Instruments Co., Ltd. Fluorescence spectra were collected using a Hitachi F-2500 fluorescence spectrophotometer. Ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) spectra were obtained using Shimadzu UV-1800 spectrophotometer. The infrared spectrum was measured by Nicolet 6700 Fourier infrared spectrometer. The size distribution of the synthesized ECD and ECDY was determined on a Malvcerv Nano S from Malvern Panalytical. Transmission electron microscopic (TEM) images were measured on a JEM-2100 electron microscope at 200 kV.

Enteromorpha Prolifera carbon dots (ECD) were synthesized according to the previously reported method [39]. Specifically, the Enteromorpha Prolifera collected from the beach was washed, smashed, and centrifuged to prepare a paste, which was then transferred into the microwave digestion system and heated at 200 °C for 30 min (500 W, 30 atm). The resulted solution was filtered (0.22 μm filter membrane) to remove the solid impurities, and then purified by ultrafiltration (10 Kd, Amicon Ultra, Millipore) and dialysis (1000 D, Amicon Ultra, Millipore) to collect Enteromorpha Prolifera carbon dots (ECD). ECDY was prepared by mixing 1 mL of ECD solution (30.0 mg mL−1), 60.0 mg of EDC, 70.0 mg of NHS, and 1.5 mL of tyrosine methyl ester (10.0 mg mL−1), and reacting for 12 h at room temperature. After the reaction the mixture was repeatedly dialyzed against ultrapure water in a dialysis bag (1000 D, Amicon Ultra, Millipore) for 24 h to remove the extra reactants and obtain a pure ECDY solution. The ECDY solution can be stored under shading at 4 °C for at least 6 months, while the spectroscopic properties are almost unchanged.

ECDY (final concentration 0.5 mg mL−1) was mixed with different tyrosinase concentration gradients (0–10.0 U mL−1) to evaluate the response of ECDY to tyrosinase. ECDY was mixed with various conventional biomolecules (1.0 mg mL−1) and enzymes (10.0 U mL−1), including glucose (GLU), glutathione (GSH), cysteine (CYS), bovine albumin (BSA), horseradish peroxidase (HRP), protamine sulfate (PRO), calf thymus DNA (CT-DNA), lysine (LYS), esterase (EST), and lysozyme (LZM) to check the selectivity and biocompatibility of ECDY. The fluorescence intensity at 465 nm of the mixture was recorded at an excitation wavelength of 390 nm. ECD (0.5 mg mL−1) was mixed with tyrosine methyl ester, tyrosinase, or 1,4-p-benzoquinone at different concentrations, to explore the fluorescence response of ECD to different substances. The steady-state fluorescence spectra were measured at an excitation wavelength of 390 nm after mixing for 20 min.

For the cell lysate measurement, human embryonic kidney cells (HEK293-A), mouse fibroblasts (NIH3T3), human lung cancer epithelial cells (A549), human liver cancer cells (HepG2), human cervical cancer cells (HeLa), mouse colon cancer cells (CT26), and mouse melanoma cells (B16) were selected. The cells were seeded in cell culture flasks with Dulbecco's Modified Eagle Medium (DMEM) culture medium (Invitrogen, USA) containing 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS). After 48 h of incubation for each cell line 1 × 106 cells were harvested and homogenized by Polytron homogenizer in PBS. The cell lysis solution (1 × 106 cells mL−1) was obtained by collecting the supernatant after centrifugation at 3000 rpm for 10 min at 4 °C. The ECDY was added to the lysis solution of different cell lines and the fluorescence intensity at 465 nm of the mixture was recorded at an excitation wavelength of 390 nm after 30 min of incubation at 37 °C.

For the whole cell imaging experiment, HEK293-A, HeLa, and B16 were seeded onto 12 mm sterile coverslips in a 24-well plate and cultured for 48 h, washed thrice with PBS, and incubated with culture medium containing ECDY (0.5 mg mL−1) at 37 °C for 4 h. After washing thrice with cold PBS, the cells were fixed for 20 min in 400 μL of 4% paraformaldehyde and rinsed thrice with PBS. After staining, fluorescent images were taken with a confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM, Nikon Al, Nikon, Japan). The ECDY were excited with a 405 nm laser and the emission was collected between 425 and 475 nm. The experimental procedures of ECD (0.5 mg mL−1) were the same as above.

Enteromorpha Prolifera carbon dots (ECD) with a large number of carboxyl groups attached on the surface were synthesized according to the microwave hydrothermal method as reported in our previous research [39]. Compared to the conventional CDs, ECD show some specific properties, e.g., the weak but clear near-infrared fluorescence emission, though most of the spectroscopic properties are quite similar. The mechanism of the photoluminescence of ECD has not yet been accurately understood, but due to the biological complexity of Enteromorpha Prolifera we believe that some trace elements might play important roles. Subsequently, 1-Ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide (EDC) and N-Hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) were used as the carboxyl activator and protective agent to link ECD with tyrosine methyl ester and the fluorescent probe ECDY was obtained (Fig. 1 A).

ECDY probes possess a stable dispersing condition in solution and can be stored at 4 °C for more than six months without any decomposition or aggregation. The high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM) image of ECDY (Fig. 1B) shows that ECDY probes are well-dispersed spheres with similar sizes and the lattice spacing of the lattice fringes is approximately 0.21 nm (the inset of Fig. 1B), which is well-matched with the in-plane lattice spacing of (100) planes in graphene (d100 = 0.213 nm) [40]. A similar structure has also been observed in ECD (Fig. S1A), indicating that the covalent modification does not change the crystal structure of ECDY. Statistics of particle size by HR-TEM (Fig. 1C) shows that the particle size of ECDY is 2.76 ± 0.55 nm, similar to free ECD (2.71 ± 0.49 nm) (Fig. S1B). The dynamic light scattering (DLS) result (Fig. 1D) shows that the average hydration diameter of ECDY is 3.39 nm, which is slightly bigger than ECD (3.16 nm), due to the coupling of surface groups. The surface difference caused by ligand conjugation is more obviously revealed by DLS measurement in solution.

ECDY solution emits bright blue fluorescence upon excitation with a UV light. Compared to the widely used fluorescent dye FITC, ECDY displays better photostability and remains about 95% of its initial intensity after 420 s of continuous laser irradiation (Fig. S2A). The excitation-dependent emission spectra are recorded (Fig. S2B) and the most efficient emission, peaked at 465 nm, is measured when the ECDY probes are excited at 390 nm (Fig. 2 A). This phenomenon is similar to the previously reported conventional carbon dots, but the mechanism is not yet clear [41]. Comparing the fluorescence spectra of ECDY to ECD, it can be seen that the fluorescence intensity at the excitation wavelength of 390 nm slightly increases after the ligand coupling reaction and the fluorescence emission peak of ECDY exhibits a hypochromic shift (Fig. 2A). According to Zhu's research, this phenomenon is most likely induced by the change in the hybridization states of the carbon skeleton and the attached chemical groups during the tyrosine methyl ester conjugations [42]. Analysis of the infrared spectra (Fig. 2B) shows that a new peak at 1650 cm−1 appears after the coupling reaction, which can be assigned to the characteristic amide–carbonyl (OC–N) stretching vibration, implying the formation of amide groups in ECDY. In addition, the UV–Vis absorption spectra of ECDY and control materials were summarized in Fig. 2C. It is found that the tyrosine absorption peak at around 275 nm (green line) appears in the spectrum of ECDY particles (red line). Comparing the spectrum of ECDY (red line) to the control material (blue line), where ECD particles are simply mixed with tyrosine methyl ester without covalent bonding, it is shown that the absorption peak near 270 nm differs significantly from each other, also verifying the successful construction of ECDY.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of ECDY to tyrosinase
ce:section.ce:para: So far, many studies have demonstrated that quinones can significantly quench the fluorescence of quantum dots [37,43,44], which was classified as photoinduced electron transfer (PET) or energy transfer between quinone and quantum dots. The mechanism is also working on the ECDY nanoparticles. When tyrosinase is added to the solution of ECDY, it selectively oxidizes the tyrosine methyl ester residue on the surfaces to dopa structure and consequently dopaquinone (Fig. S3A), which can carry out electron transfer or energy transfer within the probe particle and result in the quenched fluorescence of ECDY (Fig. 3 A).

A series of spectroscopic experiments were conducted to demonstrate the tyrosinase activity assessment capability of ECDY. Firstly, the fluorescence intensity change of ECDY upon the addition of tyrosinase was carried out in comparison with ECD. As depicted in Fig. 3B, S3B and S3C, when the highly concentrated tyrosinase (10.0 U mL−1) is added, the fluorescence of ECD keeps invariant, whereas the fluorescence of ECDY drops sharply (by 75%). The direct quenching by tyrosinase is excluded and the crucial role played by the surface ligands in ECDY is proved, i.e., only after the tyrosine residues are linked, the resulted ECDY probe shows a significant fluorescence response to tyrosinase. Fig. 3C and D indicate that the fluorescence of ECDY decreases very rapidly after the addition of tyrosinase and the reaction is completed within 20 min.

As a control, ECD, free tyrosine methyl ester, and tyrosinase were simply mixed in solution to simulate the intermolecular quenching system, which was the mechanism applied in several previously reported tyrosinase assays [32–34]. The experimental results (Figs. S4A and B) show that the fluorescence quenching phenomenon does occur in the physically mixed system, indicating that the dopaquinone produced by the tyrosinase oxidation of free tyrosine methyl ester can also carry out electron transfer or energy transfer through the intermolecular pathway to quench the fluorescence of ECD. However, the covalent attachment of tyrosine residue in ECDY brings the quencher units, dopaquinone resulted from the enzymatic reaction, into a close proximity to the fluorescent ECD units, reducing the possible energy loss during diffusion and facilitating the electron transfer [45]. Therefore, a faster fluorescence quenching response and a much more significant sensitivity towards tyrosinase are achieved.

Keeping the concentration ECDY invariant and fixing the reaction time (20 min), the fluorescence intensity of ECDY decreases gradually as the concentration of tyrosinase increases (Fig. 4 ). A good linear relationship is obtained between the fluorescence quenching effect (F/F 0) and the tyrosinase concentration over the range from 0 to 750.0 U L−1 (y = – 0.335x + 0.997, R 2 = 0.995), which should allow quantification. The calculated detection limit for tyrosinase at a signal to noise ratio of 3 is 66.7 U L−1, which is among the lowest detection limit when compared to the previous study [46].

It is known that the enzyme activity is strongly dependent on the temperature, therefore the effect of temperature on the fluorescence quenching of ECDY was conducted. The results (Fig. S5) show that between 15 °C and 40 °C when the temperature increases, the fluorescence of ECDY decays at a faster initial rate and ends in a more efficient quenching level, which could be ascribed to the enhanced enzymatic activity at higher temperature in this range. When the temperature is increased to above 40 °C, the initial fluorescence decay rate decreases and the quenching becomes less efficient, due to the deactivation of tyrosinase. Therefore the optimal operation temperature of ECDY system is around 40 °C, which fits well with the aimed application of living cells measurements.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro detection of melanoma cells
ce:section.ce:para: So far, the application of ECDY for the detection of tyrosinase in solution has been demonstrated. Compared to the ordinary solution with well-defined composition, the intracellular medium is much more complex and crowded, containing a great variety of substances, which may interfere with the tyrosinase assay. Therefore, it is very important to verify the selectivity and biocompatibility of ECDY before applying it to the living melanoma cells detection. Firstly, the fluorescence spectra of ECDY in the presence of various inorganic ions were investigated. The results(Fig. S6)show that at the concentration of 100 μM none of the tested inorganic ions make a significant impact on the fluorescence intensity of ECDY. Secondly, the fluorescence measurement carried out in buffer solutions with different pH properties proves that ECDY is able to maintain a constant emission under physiological pH conditions (Fig. S7A). When tyrosinase was added, the pH dependent experiments reveal that the best working condition of ECDY detection is in the pH range of 6–7. Both strong acid and base reduce the sensing efficiency, due to the denaturation of tyrosinase (Fig. S7B).

Additionally, 10 types of conventional biomolecules and enzymes were selected as the interference substance to verify the specificity of ECDY, including glucose (GLU), glutathione (GSH), cysteine (CYS), bovine albumin (BSA), horseradish peroxidase (HRP), protamine sulfate (PRO), calf thymus DNA (CT-DNA), lysine (LYS), esterase (EST), and lysozyme (LZM). Experimental results (Fig. 5 A) show that when ECDY is mixed with solutions containing various biomolecules at the same concentration (the concentrations of enzymes are 10.0 U mL−1 and the concentrations of other biomolecules are 1.0 mg mL−1), among others, the fluorescence of ECDY is selectively quenched by tyrosinase by around 75% due to the specific conversion of surface attached tyrosine residue to dopaquinone, inducing the intramolecular quenching. It should be noted that there are many biological molecules that can work as tyrosinase inhibitors, including biothiols, vitamin C, Kojic acid, etc [47]. Although the inhibitor itself does not vary the ECDY properties, its presence might interfere with the measurement of tyrosinase activity. On the contrary, this effect can also be employed for the tyrosinase inhibitor screening.

To verify the in vitro utility of ECDY probe, seven cell lines, including human embryonic kidney cells (HEK-293A), mouse fibroblasts (NIH3T3), human lung cancer epithelial cells (A549), mouse colon cancer cells (CT26), human cervical cancer cells (HeLa), human liver cancer cells (HepG2), and mouse melanoma cells (B16), were selected, which could be divided into three groups, non-cancerous cells with low expression of tyrosinase (HEK293-A and NIH3T3), non-melanoma cancer cells with normal expression of tyrosinase (A549, CT26, HeLa, and HepG2), and melanoma cell (B16) with overexpressed tyrosinase [31,35,48]. After culturing the cells under normal conditions for 48 h, the cell lysate solutions of different cell lines were prepared and incubated with ECDY probes for 30 min to check their FL variation. The results (Fig. 5B) show that the three groups of cell lysate exhibit very different fluorescence efficiency. Among all cell lines, the tyrosinase-negative non-cancerous group shows the weakest variation in comparison to blank control (F/F 0 = 95%–99%), tyrosinase-strongly-positive B16 cell lysate shows the most intensive quenching in their fluorescence (F/F 0 = 39%–44%), while the tyrosinase-weakly-positive cell lysate gives out a medium fluorescence change (F/F 0 = 76%–88%). In contrast, the results of control experiments, where the naked ECD particles instead of functionalized ECDY were mixed with the cell lysate solutions of seven different cell lines and incubated for 30 min, show that the fluorescence of seven samples remains invariant (Fig. S8), verifying the tyrosinase conversion related quenching mechanism.

The in vitro experimental results clearly demonstrate that the fluorescence quenching efficiency of ECDY has a good correlation with the apparent expression level of tyrosinase in different cell lines and the intensive quenching of ECDY can be applied for the specific detection of melanoma cells. According to the literature, the activity of tyrosinase in melanoma cells is about 4.0 U mL−1 [49,50], which should result in the ECDY fluorescence efficiency as 50% (readout from Fig. 4A and B), consistent with the value measured with cell lysate in Fig. 5B, further proving the feasibility of ECDY probe for the melanoma cells detection. It is worth noting that considering the complexity of cells and the presence of biogenic tyrosinase inhibitors such as biothiols, the value reported by ECDY probe is the apparent tyrosinase activity. Only when the types and concentrations of inhibitors are known, the total expression of tyrosinase can be calculated.

In order to check the sensitivity of ECDY probe to melanoma cells, a series of normal cell (NIH3T3)/melanoma cell (B16) mixtures with varied ratio were prepared and lysed (total cell density was 1 × 106 cells mL−1). ECDY probe was then added to each sample and the fluorescence emission spectra were measured after 30 min. It is shown that the fluorescence intensity of ECDY decreases gradually as the ratio of B16 cells increases. By fitting the data at low percentage to a linear function, a B16 cell detection limit of 0.1% is achieved, which corresponds to the cell density as a single cell per μL, indicating a good sensitivity of melanoma cells detection (Fig. S9).

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo melanoma cells distinguishing from others
ce:section.ce:para: We furthermore demonstrate the application of ECDY for living melanoma cell detection. Three cell lines, HEK293-A, HeLa, and B16 were selected to represent the normal cells with low expression of tyrosinase, non-melanoma cancer cells with medium expression of tyrosinase, and melanoma cells with overexpressed tyrosinase, respectively. The cells were cultured under normal condition for 48 h, then incubated with ECDY for 4 h, and scanned with laser-induced confocal microscope. As shown in Fig. 6 A, the tyrosinase-negative normal cell line (HEK293-A) displays bright fluorescence signals, whereas no significant signal is observed in the tyrosinase-strongly-positive melanoma cell line (B16), and a weak fluorescence signal is collected in the tyrosinase-weakly-positive cancer cell line (HeLa).

There are two possible factors working for the distinct weak intracellular fluorescence intensity of B16,the low concentration of probes inside the cells or the quenched luminescence efficiency. The control experiments where the cells were incubated with naked ECD particles show that the bright blue fluorescence of ECD is observed in all three cell samples with similar intensity. Considering the naked ECD particles gives out roughly the same level of FL emission when mixed with different cell lysates (Fig. 5B), it is reasonable to deduce that the intracellular concentration of ECD in different cell lines are approximately at the same level, which results from the very closed uptake efficiency of ECD particles by various cell lines. ECDY probe has a very similar hydrodynamic diameter and hydrophilicity to naked ECD, leading to the similar cellular uptake behavior, i.e., the uptake efficiency of ECD particles by different cell lines are similar to each other. Therefore the quenched luminescence efficiency related to the high tyrosinase activity level is the main working mechanism for the invisible fluorescence signal of ECDY in melanoma cells. The qualitatively different responses in Fig. 6B immediately confirm the specific recognition ability of ECDY at the in vivo cellular level.

To further illustrate the superiorities of ECDY, Table 1 sums up several previously reported tyrosinase probes. It is worth noting that ECDY stands out with a low detection limit and the cellular specimen measurement capability.

The measurement of tyrosinase activity in cells provides information for the primary diagnosis of melanoma. Although the fluorescent probes for detecting tyrosinase in solution have been extensively studied, the complex intracellular environment brings additional challenges for the living cell detection. In this study, a novel tyrosinase probe, ECDY, is developed, where Enteromorpha Prolifera is used as the raw material which offers the probe good biocompatibility and bright fluorescence, and the ligands of tyrosinase are covalently linked with the fluorescent units. This unique structure brings the quencher residues, resulted from the enzymatic reaction, into a close proximity to the fluorophore and provides a much more significant sensitivity towards tyrosinase. More importantly, the intramolecular quenching mechanism endows high specificity and enables the in vitro and in vivo detection of melanoma cells. All the experimental results demonstrate that ECDY is a promising probe for tyrosinase activity assessment, which can be applied for measurements in solution, in a complicated biological medium, in different cell lysates, and within the living melanoma cells. Furthermore, this study also offers a new strategy for the construction of functional nanomaterials, which is transferable to other enzyme activity assessment systems.

Qi Dai: Methodology, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Xiaojuan Wang: Conceptualization, Writing - review & editing, Funding acquisition, Investigation. Cai Liu: Resources. Baosheng Ge: Supervision. Xiqi Ma: Data curation. Zhixiong Zhang: Investigation, Visualization. Jinyi Yu: Validation. Xiaoqiang Wang: Writing - review & editing. Fang Huang: Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was supported by the Qingdao Municipal People's Livelihood Science and Technology Project (grant no. 17-3-3-76-nsh), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (grant no. 21673294), the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (grant no.ZR2019ZD17), the Key Technologies R&D Program of Shandong Province (grant no. 2019GSF108159), and the Research Project of Qingdao Customs (grant no. QK202035).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2021.338605.
The author(s) declare that they have no competing interests.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Malignant melanoma: genetics and therapeutics in the genomic era

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Chin, L.A. Garraway, D.E. Fisher, Malignant melanoma: genetics and therapeutics in the genomic era, Genes Dev. 20(16) (2006) 2149-2182.
